Agricultural Geography

Spatial patterns of agriculture, crop systems, land use, and food security.

Author

Geography Team

Welcome to the Agricultural Geography module of Geography OpenCourseWare. This course examines the spatial organization of agriculture — including farming systems, crop distribution, land use patterns, agricultural revolutions, and contemporary challenges of food security.

UGC NET Syllabus

  • Land capability classification and Land Use Planning.
  • Cropping Pattern: Methods of delineating crop combination regions (Weaver, Doi and Rafiullah), Crop diversification.
  • Von Thunen’s Model of Land Use Planning.
  • Measurement and Determinants of Agricultural Productivity, Regional variations in Agricultural Productivity.
  • Agricultural Systems of the World.

Agricultural regionalisation

Content for Agricultural regionalisation coming soon.

Agricultural Revolutions

Transformative shifts in agricultural systems.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

NoteRevolutions in Farming
  • First Agricultural Revolution (Neolithic): The transition from hunting-gathering to settled farming ~10,000 BCE.
  • Second Agricultural Revolution: Mechanization, crop rotation, and enclosure movement in 18th-19th century Europe.
  • Green Revolution: High-Yielding Varieties (HYVs), irrigation, chemical fertilizers, and pesticides — especially in India (1960s-70s).
  • Gene Revolution: GMOs, biotechnology, and precision agriculture.
  • Evergreen Revolution: Swaminathan’s concept — productivity without ecological harm.

Food Security & Contemporary Issues

Challenges facing global agriculture.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

ImportantCurrent Challenges
  • Global Food Security: FAO definition — availability, access, utilization, and stability.
  • Hunger & Malnutrition: Global Hunger Index, stunting, wasting, and micronutrient deficiency.
  • India’s Food Security: PDS, NFSA (2013), PM-KISAN, and buffer stock management.
  • Climate Change & Agriculture: Shifting agro-climatic zones, crop failure risk, and adaptation strategies.
  • WTO & Agriculture: Agreement on Agriculture, subsidies, market access, and developing country concerns.
  • Organic Farming: Principles, certification, and the growth of sustainable agriculture.

Books and Further Readings

Agricultural Geography provides the spatial framework for understanding food production and livelihoods. Continue with Economic Geography and Indian Physical for related content.

Fundamentals of Agricultural Geography

Agricultural geography focuses on the spatial distribution of agricultural activities and the multitude of factors that influence farming choices across different regions. It acts as the bridge between human societies and their physical environments through the lens of food production.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

NoteKey Principles
  • Factors Affecting Agriculture: Agricultural patterns are shaped by a complex interplay of physical factors (climate, soil fertility, topography, and water availability) and human factors (labor availability, capital investment, technological access, and government policies).
  • Agricultural Regions (Whittlesey): Derwent Whittlesey’s classic 1936 classification identified 13 distinct agricultural regions of the world based on crop types, livestock associations, labor intensity, and the ultimate destination of the produce.
  • Intensive vs. Extensive Agriculture: Intensive farming involves high inputs of capital and labor relative to land area (e.g., wet rice cultivation in Asia), aiming for maximum yield per hectare. Extensive farming uses vast tracts of land with relatively low inputs per area (e.g., commercial wheat farming in the American Midwest), focusing on yield per capita.
  • Subsistence vs. Commercial Agriculture: Subsistence agriculture is designed primarily to provide food for direct consumption by the farmer and their family, typical in developing nations. Commercial agriculture focuses on cash-crop production for sale off the farm, dominating the developed world.

land holdings, land tenure and land reforms

Land Use & Land Tenure

How agricultural land is owned, managed, and regulated.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

TipLand Management
  • Von Thünen’s Model: Agricultural land use as a function of distance from the market.
  • Land Tenure Systems: Owner-cultivated, tenant farming, sharecropping, and cooperative farming.
  • Land Reforms in India: Abolition of intermediaries, tenancy reforms, land ceiling legislation.
  • Land Degradation: Soil erosion, salinization, waterlogging, and desertification.
  • Precision Agriculture: GPS-guided farming, soil mapping, variable-rate technology, and drones.

Livestock resources and white revolution

Content for Livestock resources and white revolution coming soon.

Major Crops & Distribution

Spatial patterns of key agricultural commodities.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

ImportantCrop Geography
  • Rice: Monsoon Asia — India, China, Bangladesh, Indonesia, Vietnam.
  • Wheat: Mid-latitude steppes and plains — USA, India, China, Russia, France.
  • Maize: Americas, China, Africa — versatile cereal for food, feed, and biofuel.
  • Cotton: Tropical and subtropical belts — India, USA, China, Pakistan, Brazil.
  • Tea & Coffee: Tea (Assam, China, Kenya), Coffee (Brazil, Vietnam, Colombia, Ethiopia).
  • Sugarcane: Tropical regions — Brazil, India, Thailand, China.
  • Oilseeds: Soybean (USA, Brazil), Palm Oil (Indonesia, Malaysia), Groundnut (India, China).

Significance of dry farming

Content for Significance of dry farming coming soon.

Types of Farming

Different parts of the world have cultivated distinct farming systems adapted to their specific environmental constraints and economic demands.

Get the Presentation ↗   |   Watch the Video ↗

TipFarming Systems
  • Shifting Cultivation: Also known as slash-and-burn, this is the oldest form of agriculture (e.g., Jhum in India, Milpa in Central America). Farmers clear forest patches, burn the debris for nutrient-rich ash, and move to a new plot when soil fertility declines.
  • Pastoral Nomadism: A subsistence strategy based on the herding of domesticated animals over expansive dryland territories, such as the Sahel or Central Asian steppes, dictated by the seasonal availability of pasture and water.
  • Intensive Subsistence (Wet Rice): Dominant in densely populated Monsoon Asia. It is highly labor-intensive, relying on meticulous water management and terracing to maximize the yield of small landholdings.
  • Plantation Agriculture: A legacy of colonialism, involving large-scale commercial monoculture of cash crops like tea, coffee, rubber, sugarcane, and palm oil, primarily located in the tropics but owned by multinational corporations.
  • Commercial Grain Farming: Highly mechanized, extensive farming of single cereal crops (wheat, corn) on vast plains, such as the North American Prairies, the Pampas of Argentina, and the Steppes of Russia.
  • Mediterranean Agriculture: Highly specialized farming occurring along the Mediterranean Sea, California, and South Africa, focusing on high-value crops like olives, grapes, citrus fruits, and winter vegetables that thrive in mild, wet winters and hot, dry summers.
